title: Chapter 16: Bay Seven
content:
**SUBJECT: CHEN, LUCY**
**LOCATION: SUBLEVEL B — BAY 7**
**STATUS: ACTIVE**
**DAY: 19**
Two hundred and forty-seven.

The ceiling tiles are fiberboard, one foot square, laid in a grid of seventeen rows by — no. Not quite. The final row has only nine tiles instead of the full fourteen, because the northeast corner curves where a ventilation shaft cuts through. She counted it eleven times before she was sure. Two hundred and forty-seven ceiling tiles, and she has memorized the stain on tile 193 that looks like a jellyfish, and the hairline crack running from tile 72 to tile 88, and the way tile 116 sits a quarter-inch lower than its neighbors, a tiny sunken mouth in the white gypsum face above her.

Thirteen screws hold the medical chair together. Four in the base, four in the backrest, three in the armrest mounts, two in the IV pole bracket. She knows this because she has pressed her thumbnail against each one, mapping them like constellations, turning the rusted Phillips heads into stars.

Four guards. Eight-hour shifts. Handoff at midnight, 0800, 1600. The midnight guard — she calls him Sloppy, because she doesn't know his name and because he always takes forty-three seconds longer than the others to complete the corridor check — leaves the desk at 0042 to do his walk. If he's feeling lazy, it's 0044. He never, not once in nineteen days, has checked the intercom housing.
They took her Apple Watch on Day 12.

She remembers the exact moment — the guard's thick fingers unclasping the band, the sudden nakedness of her wrist, the cold air hitting skin that hadn't felt air in twelve days. She'd almost broken then. Almost lost the rhythm. Because the watch had been her instrument: she'd been slowing her heart rate in deliberate patterns, encoding Morse into her own pulse — dots and dashes of bradycardia transmitted through the watch's health monitoring to whatever cloud still received her data. Short beat, short beat, long beat. L-U. She'd gotten as far as LUCY CHEN BAY SEVEN SUBLEVEL before the anomaly flag triggered on someone's dashboard and two guards showed up.

She'd cried that night. Not from fear — from frustration. Nineteen days of controlled breathing, of box-breathing and Wim Hof and every pranayama technique she'd ever taught on camera, and they'd found her channel.
But they didn't find the backup.

Because the backup isn't a device. It isn't a frequency or a gadget or something they can confiscate. The backup is the intercom system — the speaker mounted above the bay entrance, the microphone casing bolted to the wall beside the door, the PA conduit that runs through Sublevel B and connects to the building's AV backbone on the ground floor. During shift handoffs, the intercom cycles through a ninety-second diagnostic — a click, a hum, a brief window where the mic is live and the speaker feeds to the system instead of from it.

Lucy Chen is a wellness influencer. She has 2.3 million followers on Instagram. She has taught breathwork to soccer moms in Scottsdale and meditation to tech bros in SoMa. She has said the words "optimize your wellness journey" more times than she has said "I love you," and she is aware of the irony, and she does not care, because right now those same skills — the body control, the deliberate autonomic regulation, the mindfulness that everyone thought was so *aesthetic* — are the only reason she is still sane enough to fight.
Breathe in, four counts. Hold, four counts. Out, four counts. Hold, four counts.
Her heart rate drops. Steady. Controller. She is the controller.

The fluorescent tubes above her hum at a constant 60 hertz. She knows this because on Day 3 she started humming back, matching the pitch, and the vibration in her skull became a kind of companionship. The light never changes. There is no dawn, no dusk, no golden hour — just the flat, overexposed white of a world without shadows. Perpetual clinical noon. The walls are the same blanco sterili as the ceiling, painted with something glossy that refuses to hold a mark, and the air smells of isopropyl alcohol layered over something darker — copper, old and persistent, the kind of metallic scent that doesn't dissipate because it isn't a surface smell. It's in the walls. It's in the grout between the floor tiles. It's on the chair's vinyl upholstery, which is cracked and patched in places, and which she has pressed her cheek against on nights when she couldn't sleep, feeling the cold synthetic give of it against her face, breathing in the antiseptic and the underlying blood-smell, tasting it — the faint, bitter tang of iron that coats her tongue whether she swallows or not. Blood loss does that. Shrink the volume by enough and every swallow tastes like licking a battery.

The IV drip feeds her saline and something clear that makes her limbs feel like wet sand. They change the bag twice a day. The needle site rotates between her left and right inner elbow, the skin there a quilt of bruised colors — green, yellow, the fresh purple of today's puncture. And when they take blood — every three days — the marks they leave are different.
Two neat holes. Always two. Symmetrical. Curved. Not a needle.
Teeth.

She doesn't think about what that means. She can't afford to think about what that means. Thinking about it leads to a place where breathwork can't reach her, where the box breaks and the counts collapse and her heart rate spikes past a hundred and the monitors they haven't thought to check will flag her again. So she thinks about the tiles instead. Two hundred and forty-seven. She thinks about the screws. Thirteen. She thinks about the guards and their schedules and the ninety-second window that yawns open like a mouth every eight hours, waiting for her to speak into it.
The gurney wheels squeak.

Lucy hears them before she sees them — the particular pitch of rubber on concrete, half a frequency sharper than the food cart, accompanied by a second sound that makes her stomach lock: a woman's voice, low and urgent, speaking Romanian.
*"Vă rog, vă rog, am o fiică."*
Please. Please. I have a daughter.
Lucy is on her feet before she knows she's moving. The IV tube pulls taut, a sharp reminder, and she steadies herself against the chair's armrest as the gurney passes the bars of Bay 7.

The woman is young — early twenties, maybe. Dark hair fanned across the thin pillow, face bloodless under the fluorescent glare. Her wrists are strapped to the gurney rails. Her eyes are wide, wet, the whites showing all around the iris like a cornered animal. She's speaking faster now, switching between Romanian and English: *"Please — vă rog — my daughter, she's four, please—"*
The guards — two of them, both in the dark tactical gear that Lucy has learned to read by the way it fits; the tall one moves first, the shorter one hangs back — don't slow. They never slow.

Lucy reaches through the bars. Her arm extends fully, fingers stretched, and the cold metal of the horizontal bar presses against her shoulder socket. She can't reach. The gurney is four feet away, then six, then the woman's face is turning toward the sound of Lucy's voice.
"Hey." Lucy keeps her voice low but sharp — the tone she used to use to cut through background noise on livestreams. "HEY. My name's Lucy. What's your name?"
The woman's eyes find her. Dark eyes, enormous. They track Lucy's face for one second, two — the guards haven't noticed, or don't care — and then:
"Elena."
"Elena." Lucy grips the bar. The metal is cold enough that she feels it in her molars. "Listen to me. Count everything. Keep your mind working. Don't let them take that too."

The tall guard glances sideways. Elena is already past, her gurney disappearing around the corridor bend, her voice fading — *"count everything"* — repeating it like a prayer, or maybe like Lucy gave her a number and Elena is holding it.

Lucy's hand stays on the bar. Her knuckles have gone white. She can feel the cold traveling from her fingers to her wrist to the crook of her elbow, where fresh puncture marks still throb beneath the gauze. She doesn't let go for a long time.
She doesn't think about Maya.

She hasn't let herself think about Maya in — how long? Weeks. Since before the watch was taken, probably. Since before the heart rate Morse code failed and the only channel she had left was the intercom and the slim, almost impossible chance that a signal could travel from a microphone in Sublevel B through the PA backbone to the AV network on the ground floor and that someone in that network might be listening.
But now, in the white-on-white silence of Bay 7, with Elena's voice still echoing in her inner ear and the copper taste thick on her tongue, Lucy lets herself.

Maya's terrible coffee — always the darkest roast, always burnt, always served in a mug that said ERROR 404: MOTIVATION NOT FOUND, which Lucy had found insufferable and also, God help her, charming. Maya's headphones clamped over her ears like armor, the big over-ear ones that made her look like a satellite dish, her eyes far away and her mouth doing that concentrated half-frown she wore when she was debugging something. Maya's voice when she explained a technical concept — slow, careful, patient, like she was translating from a language only she spoke into one that ordinary humans could understand. She never condescended. She just... translated.
*"Everything leaves a frequency trace, Luce. Every sound, every vibration — it's all energy, and energy doesn't disappear. It just moves. You just have to know where to listen."*

They'd been in Maya's apartment. Maya had been showing her the spectral analyzer on her laptop, pointing at spikes on a waveform with the tip of a mechanical pencil. Lucy had been half-listening, half-watching the way Maya's hands moved — quick, precise, the hands of someone who thought in systems and schemas. She'd said something like "That's so interesting" in the voice she used when she didn't understand yet but wanted to, and Maya had looked up at her, and for a fraction of a second the debugging face had softened into something else.

Lucy whispers to the empty bay: "I'm tapping into your system, M. You taught me about audio frequencies, remember? You said everything leaves a trace." Her voice cracks on the last word. She lets it. There's no one to hear. "I'm betting my life on that."

The midnight shift change begins with the sound of boots in the corridor — Sloppy's replacement arriving, the two of them talking in low voices Lucy can't make out. She's back in the chair. IV connected. Eyes half-closed, the performance of sleep she's perfected over nineteen nights.
The voices fade. The desk is unattended for four minutes during handoff while the outgoing guard briefs the incoming one in the break room. And then —
*Click.*
The intercom. The diagnostic cycle. Ninety seconds.

Lucy is out of the chair before the hum settles. The IV line yanks; she ignores the hot pull of it, the sting of the needle shifting under her skin. Three steps to the wall. Her fingers find the microphone casing — a metal box, six inches square, bolted to the wall at shoulder height. She's loosened one of the mounting screws over the past week, working it with her thumbnail, so the casing has a tiny give. Enough for her fingertip to reach the diaphragm inside.
She taps.
Dash. Dash. Dash-dot-dash. Dash-dot. (*M-A-Y-A*)
Dash-dot-dot. (*L*)
Dot-dash. (*U*)
Dash-dot-dot. (*C*)
dash-dot-dash-dot. (*Y*)
Dash-dot-dot-dot. (*B*)
dot. (*A*)
dash-dot-dash. (*Y*)
The rest follows: SEVEN — ELENA ARRIVED — NEW SHIPMENTS COMING — IPO DAY — DON'T FORGET ME.

Her fingers move with a precision she didn't know she possessed. The tremor in her wrists — twenty days of IV drips and blood draws have left her hands shaky, her fine motor control degraded — doesn't matter. She's been practicing. Every handoff, every ninety-second window, she's practiced. The first time, she got through six letters before the window closed. Now she can tap twenty-six words in under seventy seconds.
Seventy-three seconds. Seventy-four. The diagnostic cycle is ending — she can hear the pitch shifting, the hum bending upward toward the cutoff. Eighty-seven seconds.
She finishes. Three seconds to spare.

Lucy slides back to the chair. Her arm burns where the IV moved — a radiating heat that starts at the puncture and climbs to her shoulder. She presses her other hand over it, feeling the damp of blood seeping through the gauze. The smell rises: copper and iodine and something underneath that is just *her*, just human blood on sterile bandaging, the scent of being slowly, methodically drained.
She pulls the gauze back and looks at the marks.

Two neat holes. Curved. Symmetrical. The skin around them is purple-yellow, bruised in an oval that matches the shape of a mouth. A human mouth. Except the teeth aren't human — they're too uniform, too sharp, set too precisely in the arc of the bite.
Not a needle. Teeth.

She puts the gauze back. She breathes. Four in, four hold, four out, four hold. The burning fades to a throb. The throb fades to a dull ache. She has had nineteen days to learn that pain can be managed if you give it boundaries: a shape, a duration, a perimeter. The wellness voice in her head — the one that used to say *you are enough* and *breathe through the discomfort* — sounds different now. Grittier. More honest. *Breathe through this or you die.*

Lucy lies back on the medical chair. The vinyl is cold against her shoulder blades. She stares at the ceiling — tile 247, the corner tile with the vent shaft cutout, the one that looks like a missing tooth in the grid.

She thinks about her last Instagram post. Eighty thousand likes. A carousel of photos: her in lotus pose at sunrise, her holding a green smoothie, her in athletic wear against a white studio wall, the caption reading *optimize my wellness journey ✨🧘‍♀️💚* and a string of hashtags that made her cringe even as she typed them. She'd been on auto-pilot. Content machine. Smile, pose, caption, post, engage, repeat.

A bitter laugh escapes her. It sounds wrong in this room — too human, too loud, swallowed by the acoustic tiles and the 60-hertz hum and the endless white light. *Optimize my wellness journey.* She is thirty feet underground in a medical bay with bars on the entrance, being slowly exsanguinated by something with teeth, and her wellness journey consists of slowing her heart rate to send Morse code through a microphone that nobody should be able to hear.
She is optimizing the *shit* out of her wellness journey.

The laugh dies. Lucy straightens in the chair — a deliberate motion, a choice, the same way she chooses to breathe and count and tap. She is going to get out. Not because she's brave. She is not brave. She is terrified in a way that lives in her bones, a low-frequency hum beneath the fluorescent one, vibrating in a register that never quite stops. She is going to get out because she is *choosing* to fight, and there is a difference between bravery and choice, and the difference is this: bravery doesn't admit fear. Choice sits with it, feels it, knows it intimately, and acts anyway.

She reaches down and places her fingertips against the metal frame of the chair. Not the intercom this time. Something simpler. Older. The frame itself, bolted to the floor, connected to the sublevel's structural steel, connected to the building's skeleton, connected to every girder and conduit and cable running through thirty feet of concrete and rebar up to the ground floor and beyond.
She taps.
Dot-dot. (*I*)
dash-dot-dash. (*M*)
dot-dot-dot. (*S*)
dash. (*T*)
dot-dot-dash-dash. (*I*)
dash-dot-dash-dash. (*L*)
dot. (*L*)
dot-dot-dot-dot. (*H*)
dot. (*E*)
dash-dot-dot. (*R*)
dot. (*E*)
I'M STILL HERE.

The taps travel. Tiny vibrations, frequencies below the range of human hearing, propagating through the frame into the floor into the steel into the building's nervous system. She doesn't know if this works. She doesn't know if anyone is listening on this channel, if there's a receiver sensitive enough to catch the resonance of a woman's fingertips on a chair frame in Sublevel B.

But somewhere above her — past the concrete, past the rebar, past the cable trays and the HVAC runs and the humming electrical guts of a building that doesn't know it's being haunted from within — a frequency trace drifts through a cable adapter. A tiny transmitter, no bigger than a thumbnail, soldered into an AV junction box by hands that were steady and careful and translated from a language only they spoke. A device that shouldn't exist. Listening to sounds that shouldn't matter. A woman's tap-tap-tap, attenuated through thirty feet of building material, reduced to a whisper of mechanical vibration that the transmitter catches and amplifies and pushes into the network like a small, stubborn signal flare.
*Don't forget me.*
Lucy closes her eyes.

Not in defeat. Not in surrender. In rest — the strategic, deliberate rest of someone who knows exactly what the next ninety seconds demand and intends to be ready for them. Guard check in ninety seconds. Handoff at 0800, diagnostic window at 0802. Elena is in the system now — a new name, a new signal, a daughter who is four years old and waiting somewhere in Romania for a mother who may not come back unless Lucy Chen, wellness influencer, 2.3 million followers, counts the tiles and taps the codes and refuses, *refuses*, to be silent.
She breathes. Four in. Four hold. Four out. Four hold.
The fluorescent light hums.
Two hundred and forty-seven tiles. Thirteen screws. Ninety seconds.
She'll need them.
